VS Code agent path: .github/skills/real-estate-local-services-service-quote-risk-review/SKILL.md

Create the folder if needed, then write SKILL.md with this exact content:

```markdown
---
name: real-estate-local-services-service-quote-risk-review
description: >
  Review operational risk for service quotes: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "service quotes risk-review", /real-estate-local-services-service-quote-risk-review.
metadata:
  category: local-services
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Business & Revenue
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /real-estate-local-services-service-quote-risk-review

Review operational risk for service quotes: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "service quotes risk-review", /real-estate-local-services-service-quote-risk-review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this to audit a specific quote before it goes out - or the business's quote template and habits - for the failure modes that turn estimates into losses and disputes: missing exclusions, unpriced unknowns, no validity window, verbal promises the paper doesn't back, and scope described so loosely that "done" is arguable. Run it on any quote above the business's comfort threshold, on a new job type, or after a job just went badly over budget.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The quote document verbatim, plus the site-visit notes or photos it was built from (or the admission there was no site visit)
- The cost buildup behind the number: labor hours, materials pricing date, subs, permits assumed or excluded
- What was said to the customer verbally that isn't in the document
- Job-specific unknowns: what can't be inspected until work opens up
- Payment structure: deposit, progress payments, final; and validity window if any
- Jurisdiction items to verify: written-contract thresholds, deposit limits, permit responsibilities, license display requirements

## Workflow

1. Check scope definiteness first: can a stranger read the scope section and know exactly what "finished" looks like - areas, quantities, materials grade, surface prep, cleanup? Every vague noun ("repair the fence") is a dispute seed; flag it with the specific rewrite ("replace 12 pickets and two rails on the north fence section, cedar, primed and painted to match").
2. Hunt unpriced exposure: unknowns visible in site notes but absent from the quote (no exclusion, no contingency, no T&M carve-out); materials priced from stale lists; standard forgotten lines - disposal, travel, equipment rental, permit fees; and taxes handled inconsistently.
3. Check the protective clauses: validity window (a quote with no expiry is an open option against the business as materials prices move), change-order clause, deposit/payment schedule, and exclusions written for this job's actual unknowns rather than generic boilerplate.
4. Compare paper to promises: anything the estimator said on site ("we'll throw in the haul-away") must appear in the document or be flagged as an unwritten liability. Verbal scope is real scope in the customer's memory.
5. Screen compliance exposure without asserting rules: jobs above certain amounts may require specific written-contract elements, deposit caps, or notice language in some jurisdictions; permits may be legally the contractor's responsibility; work may exceed the license class held. Flag each as "verify for your jurisdiction/trade before sending."
6. Rate each risk on likelihood and impact - underpriced hidden conditions and undefined scope are the classic high/high pair - and assign each mitigation a named owner and a fix-by point (before send / before signing / before work starts).
7. Split findings into blocking (quote must not go out until fixed) and advisory, and end with the re-review gate: who checks the corrected quote before it's sent.

## Output format

**Objective** - one sentence: which quote or quoting practice is under review.

**Inputs used** - quote version, site-visit basis, cost buildup availability.

**Risk review**

| Risk | Where it appears (quoted line or gap) | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation (specific rewrite, clause, or price fix) | Owner |

Group rows: Scope definiteness / Unpriced exposure / Missing protections / Paper-vs-verbal gaps / Jurisdiction items to verify. Below the table: **Blocking findings** (fix before send) and the corrected-language suggestions for each.

**Risks / assumptions** - including anything unverifiable without a site visit.

**Next action** - fix owner, re-review, send gate.

## Do

- Point at exact lines and gaps - "no validity window anywhere in the document" is a finding; "tighten the quote" is not
- Write the replacement language for every blocking finding, ready to paste into the quote
- Treat a missing site visit on opaque scope as a finding in itself, with "quote as T&M or visit first" as the mitigation
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty
- Make the result usable in ChatGPT, Grok, Claude, Cursor, Gemini, or a human workflow

## Don't

- Don't pass a quote because the number "feels right" - the review checks structure and evidence, not vibes
- Don't assert contract-law, deposit-cap, or permit rules for the jurisdiction; flag them "verify with your licensing board/attorney"
- Don't let boilerplate exclusions stand in for job-specific ones when the site notes name specific unknowns
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions - a quote with blocking findings does not reach the customer

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every finding cites the exact line or names the exact missing element, with paste-ready replacement language for blockers
- [ ] Scope vagueness, unpriced unknowns, and missing validity/change-order/deposit terms were each explicitly checked
- [ ] Verbal promises were compared against the document, or flagged as unknown
- [ ] All jurisdiction-dependent items are marked "verify locally," none asserted
- [ ] The user can paste the output directly into their tool or workflow
- [ ] Assumptions and risks are visible
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate - corrected quote re-reviews before send
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /real-estate-local-services-service-quote-risk-review or describe the task from the skill description.

VS Code agent path: .github/skills/real-estate-local-services-service-quote-stakeholder-brief/SKILL.md

Create the folder if needed, then write SKILL.md with this exact content:

```markdown
---
name: real-estate-local-services-service-quote-stakeholder-brief
description: >
  Prepare stakeholder communication for service quotes: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "service quotes stakeholder-brief", /real-estate-local-services-service-quote-stakeholder-brief.
metadata:
  category: local-services
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Business & Revenue
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /real-estate-local-services-service-quote-stakeholder-brief

Prepare stakeholder communication for service quotes: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "service quotes stakeholder-brief", /real-estate-local-services-service-quote-stakeholder-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a quote needs to be explained to a decision-maker in plain language - the customer's cover message that walks them through scope, price basis, and exclusions; or the internal brief that gets the owner to approve an unusual quote (below margin floor, new job type, big contingency) before it goes out. The quote document carries the terms; this brief makes them understandable so the decision is fast and the surprises are zero.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Who the stakeholder is: the paying customer, a property manager approving for an owner, or the business owner reviewing internally - the brief reads differently for each
- The finished quote document (scope, price, exclusions, validity window, deposit terms) - the brief summarizes it, never replaces it
- What the site visit found, including anything the customer doesn't yet know about (the rot under the deck boards)
- The price basis in one line: what drives the number (labor days, materials grade, access difficulty)
- Competing-bid context, if known, and the customer's stated priorities (speed vs. cost vs. quality)
- Decision deadline and the quote's validity window

## Workflow

1. Open with the job in the stakeholder's words: what they asked for, restated in one sentence so they see they were heard.
2. Summarize what the inspection found, including unwelcome findings, stated plainly and early - a customer who learns about the subfloor rot from the invoice becomes a bad review; one who learns it from the brief becomes a change-order signature.
3. Explain the price in drivers, not a defensive breakdown: "most of this cost is two days of licensed labor plus the tile grade you chose" travels better than a wall of line items; the itemization lives in the attached quote.
4. Walk the boundaries explicitly: what is included, what is excluded and why (the unknowns behind the wall), what happens if an exclusion becomes real work (change order with written approval before proceeding - no surprise charges).
5. State the practical terms in one block: validity window with its reason (materials pricing), deposit and payment schedule, start-window if they approve by the deadline, and license/insurance status where customers expect it (verify what must appear on estimates in the jurisdiction).
6. Close with one clear next step: approve by [date] via signature/reply, or ask questions via [named person, direct channel]. For internal briefs, the ask is instead: approve sending this quote as-is / adjust these terms.
7. Gate it: this brief and its quote are drafts until the owner reviews them - nothing customer-facing sends without that pass.

## Output format

**Objective** - one sentence: which quote is being communicated and to whom.

**Inputs used** - quote version, site findings, customer priorities.

**Stakeholder brief**

- What you asked for - one-sentence restatement of the job
- What we found - inspection summary, including any bad news, in plain words
- The price and what drives it - number + two or three real drivers; itemization referenced to the attached quote
- What's included / excluded / what happens if - boundaries and the change-order promise (written approval before any extra work)
- Terms - validity window, deposit/payments, start timing, license/insurance note (verify jurisdiction display requirements)
- Ask - approve by [date] or contact [name, channel]

**Risks / assumptions** - e.g., pricing assumptions, access assumptions.

**Next action** - owner review gate, then send; log the follow-up date.

## Do

- Deliver bad news from the site visit in the brief, before money changes hands - it is the cheapest moment it will ever be delivered
- Keep the customer version under a page; the quote document carries the detail

# /real-estate-local-services-service-quote-stakeholder-brief

Prepare stakeholder communication for service quotes: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "service quotes stakeholder-brief", /real-estate-local-services-service-quote-stakeholder-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a quote needs to be explained to a decision-maker in plain language - the customer's cover message that walks them through scope, price basis, and exclusions; or the internal brief that gets the owner to approve an unusual quote (below margin floor, new job type, big contingency) before it goes out. The quote document carries the terms; this brief makes them understandable so the decision is fast and the surprises are zero.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Who the stakeholder is: the paying customer, a property manager approving for an owner, or the business owner reviewing internally - the brief reads differently for each
- The finished quote document (scope, price, exclusions, validity window, deposit terms) - the brief summarizes it, never replaces it
- What the site visit found, including anything the customer doesn't yet know about (the rot under the deck boards)
- The price basis in one line: what drives the number (labor days, materials grade, access difficulty)
- Competing-bid context, if known, and the customer's stated priorities (speed vs. cost vs. quality)
- Decision deadline and the quote's validity window

## Workflow

1. Open with the job in the stakeholder's words: what they asked for, restated in one sentence so they see they were heard.
2. Summarize what the inspection found, including unwelcome findings, stated plainly and early - a customer who learns about the subfloor rot from the invoice becomes a bad review; one who learns it from the brief becomes a change-order signature.
3. Explain the price in drivers, not a defensive breakdown: "most of this cost is two days of licensed labor plus the tile grade you chose" travels better than a wall of line items; the itemization lives in the attached quote.
4. Walk the boundaries explicitly: what is included, what is excluded and why (the unknowns behind the wall), what happens if an exclusion becomes real work (change order with written approval before proceeding - no surprise charges).
5. State the practical terms in one block: validity window with its reason (materials pricing), deposit and payment schedule, start-window if they approve by the deadline, and license/insurance status where customers expect it (verify what must appear on estimates in the jurisdiction).
6. Close with one clear next step: approve by [date] via signature/reply, or ask questions via [named person, direct channel]. For internal briefs, the ask is instead: approve sending this quote as-is / adjust these terms.
7. Gate it: this brief and its quote are drafts until the owner reviews them - nothing customer-facing sends without that pass.

## Output format

**Objective** - one sentence: which quote is being communicated and to whom.

**Inputs used** - quote version, site findings, customer priorities.

**Stakeholder brief**

- What you asked for - one-sentence restatement of the job
- What we found - inspection summary, including any bad news, in plain words
- The price and what drives it - number + two or three real drivers; itemization referenced to the attached quote
- What's included / excluded / what happens if - boundaries and the change-order promise (written approval before any extra work)
- Terms - validity window, deposit/payments, start timing, license/insurance note (verify jurisdiction display requirements)
- Ask - approve by [date] or contact [name, channel]

**Risks / assumptions** - e.g., pricing assumptions, access assumptions.

**Next action** - owner review gate, then send; log the follow-up date.

## Do

- Deliver bad news from the site visit in the brief, before money changes hands - it is the cheapest moment it will ever be delivered
- Keep the customer version under a page; the quote document carries the detail
- Make the change-order promise explicit ("no extra work without your written OK") - it is the single most trust-building sentence in the brief
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty
- Make the result usable in ChatGPT, Grok, Claude, Cursor, Gemini, or a human workflow

## Don't

- Don't let the brief promise anything the quote document doesn't contain - the two must match line for line on scope, price, and terms
- Don't bury or soften exclusions; an exclusion the customer didn't register is a dispute scheduled for later
- Don't pressure ("prices go up Monday") beyond the honest validity window already in the quote
- Don't assert jurisdiction rules on estimates or deposits - mark them "verify locally"
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Brief and quote document agree exactly on scope, price, exclusions, and terms
- [ ] Any adverse site finding appears in the brief in plain language
- [ ] The change-order rule (written approval before extra work) is stated
- [ ] Validity window and deposit terms are present; jurisdiction display requirements marked "verify"
- [ ] The brief is flagged as propose-before-send - owner reviews before the customer sees it
- [ ] The user can paste the output directly into their tool or workflow
- [ ] Assumptions and risks are visible
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate

description: >
  Write a decision memo for local SEO pages: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "local SEO pages decision-memo", /real-estate-local-services-local-seo-page-decision-memo.
metadata:

  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Business & Revenue
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /real-estate-local-services-local-seo-page-decision-memo

Write a decision memo for local SEO pages: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "local SEO pages decision-memo", /real-estate-local-services-local-seo-page-decision-memo.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a business must decide how to structure a location or service-area page for search - one broad page vs. a page per city, how much service-area detail to include, whether to add a new neighborhood page at all. Best fit: a multi-town service business or an agent covering several suburbs deciding where to invest page-building effort, especially when a past attempt drew a thin-content or spam impression rather than rankings.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The service area: specific cities/neighborhoods actually served, not an aspirational radius
- What proof exists per area: completed jobs, closed transactions, reviews naming the location, before/after photos tied to a real address or neighborhood
- Current page(s), if any: URL, word count, whether it reads as templated boilerplate with a city name swapped in
- Competitors' pages ranking locally, at a glance (what they include that this page doesn't)
- Platform/CMS constraints: who can publish, whether templated multi-location pages are technically easy or a manual slog

## Workflow

1. State the ranking-and-conversion goal in one sentence: which service area(s) need to show up for which search terms, and what a visitor should do on landing.
2. Lay out the structural options with real tradeoffs: (a) one strong area-overview page - easier to maintain, weaker for hyper-local queries; (b) a page per city/neighborhood - better local match, but only defensible with genuine per-area content and a real risk of thin duplicate pages if proof runs out; (c) a hybrid - one hub page plus dedicated pages only for the areas with enough real proof to fill them.
3. Score each area against a proof threshold: does it have real reviews, completed jobs, or before/after material tied to that specific place? An area without proof gets folded into the hub page, not given a thin templated page - templated city-swapped pages read as spam to both readers and search engines.
4. Flag the keyword-stuffing trap explicitly: repeating a city name unnaturally, listing every conceivable neighboring town, or stacking service+city combinations without real content between them. The recommendation must specify natural language density, not a keyword target.
5. Recommend the structure, named pages, and the proof each page will lean on. Note what's genuinely available today vs. what needs to be gathered first (e.g., "wait for three more reviews naming Riverside before publishing that page").
6. State the review/compliance angle: no page implies a valuation, appraisal, or guaranteed outcome ("your home is worth X," "we'll sell in 10 days") - mark any such language for removal or a disclaimer.
7. End with the approval gate: proposed page copy and structure go to the business owner before publishing; nothing is treated as live until approved.

## Output format

**Objective** - one sentence: which area(s)/pages this decision covers.

**Inputs used** - service area, proof inventory per area, current page state, platform constraints.

**Decision memo**

- Decision needed - one hub page / page-per-area / hybrid
- Options - all three, each with the specific tradeoff for this business
- Evidence - proof-per-area scoring, competitor gaps observed
- Recommendation - chosen structure, named pages, and what proof each will use

**Risks / assumptions** - including thin-content and spam-signal risk on any under-proofed page.

**Next action** - who drafts, who approves, and the publish gate.

## Do

- Require real, area-specific proof (reviews, jobs, photos) before recommending a dedicated page for that area
- Recommend folding thin areas into a hub page rather than publishing a templated stub
- Name the natural-language standard directly - write for the reader first, search engines second
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty
- Make the result usable in ChatGPT, Grok, Claude, Cursor, Gemini, or a human workflow

## Don't

- Don't recommend a service area the business doesn't actually serve, even if it would rank well
- Don't approve page structures that repeat city/service keyword combinations without substantive content
- Don't include valuation, appraisal, or guaranteed-outcome language - flag it "verify with a licensed professional" or remove it
- Don't describe the type of person or family who "should" want the property or service - describe the property, service, and area only
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions - no page structure is final without owner sign-off

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every recommended dedicated page has real area-specific proof behind it; areas without proof are folded into a hub page
- [ ] No keyword-stuffing pattern (unnatural city repetition, template-swapped stubs) survives into the recommendation
- [ ] No valuation, appraisal, or guaranteed-result claims appear without a "verify with a licensed professional" flag
- [ ] No demographic or "ideal buyer/family" language appears anywhere in the memo
- [ ] The user can paste the output directly into their tool or workflow
- [ ] Assumptions and risks are visible
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate - publishing requires owner sign-off
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /real-estate-local-services-local-seo-page-decision-memo or describe the task from the skill description.

---
name: real-estate-local-services-local-seo-page-operating-plan
description: >
  Build an operating plan for local SEO pages: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "local SEO pages operating-plan", /real-estate-local-services-local-seo-page-operating-plan.
metadata:
  category: local-services
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Business & Revenue
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /real-estate-local-services-local-seo-page-operating-plan

Build an operating plan for local SEO pages: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "local SEO pages operating-plan", /real-estate-local-services-local-seo-page-operating-plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when the business has decided to build or refresh location/service pages and needs an execution plan: which pages get built in what order, who gathers proof, who writes, who reviews for spam patterns and compliance language, and how pages get maintained after launch. Best fit: rolling out 3+ area pages over several weeks rather than a single one-off page.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The prioritized list of areas/pages to build (from a prior decision memo, or set here by proof availability)
- Who can gather local proof (reviews, job photos, before/after material) per area, and who writes copy
- Publishing platform and who has access to push pages live
- Current page inventory, if any, including known thin or duplicate pages needing a rewrite or takedown
- Review/approval chain: who checks for keyword-stuffing and compliance language before publish
- Timeline pressure and any seasonal factor (e.g., a service that peaks in spring)

## Workflow

1. Order the page list by proof-readiness, not aspiration: areas with reviews, completed jobs, or photos ready today go first; areas needing more proof get a "gather" task before a "write" task.
2. Assign one owner per page for proof-gathering (pulling real reviews/photos tied to that area) and one owner for drafting - these can be the same person, but the plan states it explicitly so nothing stalls waiting on an unnamed "someone."
3. Set the content standard each draft must meet before review: natural language (no unnatural city-name repetition), at least one piece of area-specific proof, a clear service description, and no valuation/appraisal/guaranteed-outcome claims.
4. Build the review gate: a named reviewer checks each draft against the content standard and against fair-marketing language (no demographic "ideal buyer" framing, no unverifiable claims) before it goes to the business owner for final publish approval.
5. Sequence publishing to avoid a spam signal: stagger launches rather than publishing a dozen near-identical pages the same day, and cross-link pages naturally (area page to hub page) rather than through footer keyword lists.
6. Plan the maintenance cadence: a periodic check (e.g., quarterly) that each page's proof is still current - stale "as of last year" reviews or outdated photos get refreshed or the page gets folded back into the hub.
7. Add a retirement rule: any page that never accumulates real area-specific proof after a set window gets merged into the hub page rather than left live as a thin stub.

## Output format

**Objective** - one sentence: the page rollout being executed and over what timeframe.

**Inputs used** - prioritized page list, proof inventory, platform access, review chain.

**Operating plan**

| Workstream | Owner | Deadline | Metric | Risk |

Rows: proof-gathering per page, drafting per page, content-standard review, compliance/spam-pattern review, staged publishing, quarterly proof refresh, thin-page retirement.

**Risks / assumptions** - including the risk of publishing before proof exists.

**Next action** - first page to build and its proof-gathering owner.

## Do

- Sequence pages by proof-readiness, not by which area the business wishes ranked highest
- Name a reviewer whose job includes catching keyword-stuffing and unverifiable claims before publish
- Build in a maintenance cadence so pages don't go stale and look abandoned
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty
- Make the result usable in ChatGPT, Grok, Claude, Cursor, Gemini, or a human workflow

## Don't

- Don't schedule a page for an area with no real proof; add a proof-gathering task ahead of it instead
- Don't let drafts skip the compliance/spam-pattern review on a deadline crunch
- Don't publish a batch of near-identical city pages simultaneously - stagger and vary the content
- Don't allow valuation, appraisal, or guaranteed-outcome claims to reach publish without a "verify with a licensed professional" flag or removal
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions - publishing requires the named final approver

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every page has a named proof-gatherer and a named drafter
- [ ] The content standard (natural language, real proof, no unverifiable claims) is stated as a gate, not a suggestion
- [ ] Publishing is staggered, not dumped, to avoid a spam signal
- [ ] A maintenance/refresh cadence and a thin-page retirement rule both appear
- [ ] No demographic or "ideal buyer/family" language appears anywhere in the plan
- [ ] The user can paste the output directly into their tool or workflow
- [ ] Assumptions and risks are visible
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate - publishing needs a named final sign-off
```

7. Add a retirement rule: any page that never accumulates real area-specific proof after a set window gets merged into the hub page rather than left live as a thin stub.

## Output format

**Objective** - one sentence: the page rollout being executed and over what timeframe.

**Inputs used** - prioritized page list, proof inventory, platform access, review chain.

**Operating plan**

| Workstream | Owner | Deadline | Metric | Risk |

Rows: proof-gathering per page, drafting per page, content-standard review, compliance/spam-pattern review, staged publishing, quarterly proof refresh, thin-page retirement.

**Risks / assumptions** - including the risk of publishing before proof exists.

**Next action** - first page to build and its proof-gathering owner.

## Do

- Sequence pages by proof-readiness, not by which area the business wishes ranked highest
- Name a reviewer whose job includes catching keyword-stuffing and unverifiable claims before publish
- Build in a maintenance cadence so pages don't go stale and look abandoned
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty
- Make the result usable in ChatGPT, Grok, Claude, Cursor, Gemini, or a human workflow

## Don't

- Don't schedule a page for an area with no real proof; add a proof-gathering task ahead of it instead
- Don't let drafts skip the compliance/spam-pattern review on a deadline crunch
- Don't publish a batch of near-identical city pages simultaneously - stagger and vary the content
- Don't allow valuation, appraisal, or guaranteed-outcome claims to reach publish without a "verify with a licensed professional" flag or removal
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions - publishing requires the named final approver

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every page has a named proof-gatherer and a named drafter
- [ ] The content standard (natural language, real proof, no unverifiable claims) is stated as a gate, not a suggestion
- [ ] Publishing is staggered, not dumped, to avoid a spam signal
- [ ] A maintenance/refresh cadence and a thin-page retirement rule both appear
- [ ] No demographic or "ideal buyer/family" language appears anywhere in the plan
- [ ] The user can paste the output directly into their tool or workflow
- [ ] Assumptions and risks are visible
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate - publishing needs a named final sign-off

# /real-estate-local-services-local-seo-page-risk-review

Review operational risk for local SEO pages: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "local SEO pages risk-review", /real-estate-local-services-local-seo-page-risk-review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this to audit an existing or drafted local SEO page before it publishes, or to sweep a live site's location pages for accumulated problems: keyword-stuffing patterns, thin duplicate content across areas, generic claims with no local proof behind them, and language that could read as discriminatory or as an unverified guarantee. Run it before launching new area pages or as a periodic site audit.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The page(s) under review, verbatim, or the live URLs
- The proof actually available for each page's claimed area (reviews, completed jobs, photos)
- Whether multiple area pages share a template, and how much text differs between them
- Any claims about pricing, valuation, timelines, or outcomes made on the page
- Platform/CMS ownership: who can edit or take down a page if the review flags it

## Workflow

1. Scan each page for keyword-stuffing signals: unnatural repetition of the city/service name, a "service areas" list stacked without content, or a title/meta tag that reads as a keyword string rather than a sentence.
2. Compare pages across areas for duplicate-content risk: if two city pages differ only by a swapped place name, flag it - search engines and readers both discount templated stubs, and it can suppress the whole page set's ranking.
3. Check every claim against available proof: testimonials, before/after photos, and "trusted in [area]" language must trace to something real and ideally attributable; generic unverifiable claims ("the area's top-rated team") without backing are findings.
4. Flag compliance-adjacent language: valuation or appraisal implications ("your home could be worth..."), guaranteed timelines or outcomes ("we'll sell your house in 2 weeks"), and any demographic-targeted phrasing describing the kind of buyer, family, or resident who "should" choose this area or service - flag all of these regardless of intent, since fair-marketing and fair-housing exposure applies even to indirect phrasing.
5. Check technical/trust signals loosely (without asserting platform-specific ranking mechanics): does the page make it easy to contact the business, is the service area stated plainly and honestly, are reviews and proof genuinely tied to that page's area.
6. For each finding, write the specific fix: the exact phrase to cut or rewrite, the proof to add, or the page to merge into a hub page. Separate blocking findings (must fix before publish, or should fix on a live page promptly) from lower-priority polish.

## Output format

**Objective** - one sentence: which page(s) are under review and why now.

**Inputs used** - pages reviewed, proof inventory, live vs. draft status.

**Risk review**

| Risk | Where it appears (quoted phrase or page) | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation (specific edit) | Owner |

Group rows: Keyword-stuffing & duplicate content / Unverified or generic claims / Compliance & fair-marketing language / Trust & contact-ease gaps. Below the table: **Blocking findings** (fix before publish, or fix now on a live page).

**Risks / assumptions** - including ranking impact being inherently uncertain and platform-dependent.

**Next action** - fix owner and re-check date before publish or after edits.

## Do

- Quote the exact offending phrase for every finding and pair it with a specific rewrite
- Treat demographic "ideal buyer/family" phrasing as a blocking finding, not a style note
- Treat duplicate-content across area pages as a ranking risk to the whole page set, not just one page
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty
- Make the result usable in ChatGPT, Grok, Claude, Cursor, Gemini, or a human workflow

## Don't

- Don't clear a page because its claims sound plausible - require actual traceable proof, not plausibility
- Don't assert specific search-ranking mechanics or guaranteed ranking outcomes - mark ranking effects "uncertain, platform-dependent"
- Don't treat valuation or guaranteed-outcome language as acceptable with a disclaimer buried in fine print - flag it clearly and recommend "verify with a licensed professional" framing or removal
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions - page edits or takedowns go through the named owner

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every finding quotes the offending phrase and includes a specific rewrite or fix
- [ ] Demographic/"ideal buyer or family" language is flagged as blocking wherever it appears
- [ ] Duplicate/templated content across area pages is checked, not just single-page issues
- [ ] Valuation, appraisal, and guaranteed-outcome claims are flagged with a "verify with a licensed professional" note
- [ ] The user can paste the output directly into their tool or workflow
- [ ] Assumptions and risks are visible
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate - no live page edit or takedown without sign-off
```

# /real-estate-local-services-local-seo-page-stakeholder-brief

Prepare stakeholder communication for local SEO pages: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "local SEO pages stakeholder-brief", /real-estate-local-services-local-seo-page-stakeholder-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a marketing lead, agent, or owner needs a fast, non-technical update on the local-page project: what pages are proposed or drafted, what's ready to publish, what's still missing proof, and what needs their approval before it goes live. Best fit: someone who won't read a full audit or plan but needs to say yes/no/wait.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The page(s) in question: area, current status (drafted, reviewed, live), and any pending edits
- The proof behind the page: what's real and included, what's still missing
- Any flags from a prior risk review (keyword-stuffing, compliance language, thin content) and their fix status
- Deadline pressure: is there a reason this needs a decision by a specific date
- Who has final publish authority

## Workflow

1. Lead with the ask in one sentence: which page(s), what stage they're at, and what decision is needed now (approve to publish / provide more proof / hold).
2. State what's ready in plain terms: the area covered, the real proof behind it (e.g., "twelve reviews naming this neighborhood, three job photos"), and confirmation that language has been checked for spam patterns and unverifiable claims.
3. Flag anything still open: missing proof for a section, a compliance note still needing a "verify with a licensed professional" line, or a keyword-stuffing pattern caught in review and already fixed or still pending a fix.
4. Keep it non-technical: skip ranking-mechanics jargon; say what the page is for and what it says, not how search algorithms might treat it.
5. Make the ask binary and dated: approve to publish by [date], or return specific feedback. Note that any requested edits get one more compliance/spam check before going live.
6. If multiple pages are covered, close with a simple status table so the stakeholder can scan status at a glance rather than reading prose for each one.

## Output format

**Objective** - one sentence: the page(s) and the decision requested.

**Inputs used** - page status, proof summary, prior review flags.

**Stakeholder brief**

- Situation - page(s), area, current stage
- What's ready - proof included, checks passed
- What's still open - missing proof, pending fixes
- Ask - approve to publish by [date], or return feedback

**Risks / assumptions** - e.g., ranking outcomes are uncertain and platform-dependent.

**Next action** - publish owner and date, or revision owner and date.

## Do

- Keep the ask binary and dated so the stakeholder isn't left guessing what "review this" means
- Summarize proof concretely (counts, specifics) rather than vaguely ("good social proof")
- Note explicitly that any edits get re-checked before publishing, so approval isn't seen as final-final
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty
- Make the result usable in ChatGPT, Grok, Claude, Cursor, Gemini, or a human workflow

## Don't

- Don't bury the ask in background - lead with what decision is needed
- Don't promise ranking results or timelines the business can't control
- Don't gloss over an unresolved compliance or spam-pattern flag just to look publish-ready
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions - no page publishes without the named approver's sign-off

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The ask is stated in the first two sentences and is binary with a date
- [ ] Proof is summarized with specifics, not vague adjectives
- [ ] Any open compliance or spam-pattern flags are visible, not hidden
- [ ] No ranking guarantee or timeline promise appears anywhere in the brief
- [ ] The user can paste the output directly into their tool or workflow
- [ ] Assumptions and risks are visible
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate - publishing needs the named approver
```

# /real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-decision-memo

Write a decision memo for client follow-up messages: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "client follow-up messages decision-memo", /real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-decision-memo.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a specific contact needs a follow-up judgment call, not a template: the buyer who went quiet after three showings, the seller lead who said "maybe in the spring," the estimate that's been sitting two weeks, or the prospect a second agent may now be working with. The memo decides whether to follow up, on what channel, with what angle - and sometimes recommends stopping, which is a legitimate outcome.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The contact history, dated: every touch so far, who initiated, what was said, and what the last message promised or asked
- What changed since the last touch that could carry a message: a new comparable sale near the home they liked, a price reduction, a crew opening next week, the quote's validity window nearing expiry
- Signals from their side: opened-but-unanswered vs. silent, "not now" vs. nothing, any stated preference on channel or timing
- Any stop conditions in play: an opt-out, a "we went another direction," or - for real estate - signs they are now represented by another agent (which changes what's appropriate; verify obligations with your broker)
- The realistic goal of one more message: appointment, decision, or a clean close-out
- Who sends it and who should approve it before it goes

## Workflow

1. Establish where this contact actually stands, from evidence: engaged-but-busy, politely done, or genuinely undecided. The message history usually says which - three unanswered value-free check-ins reads differently than one unanswered detailed question.
2. Decide whether a follow-up is warranted at all. Rules of thumb worth writing down: follow-ups carrying new, relevant information ("a home two doors from the one you liked just sold - it changes the pricing picture") outperform check-ins ("just circling back!"); a quote near its validity expiry justifies exactly one factual nudge; a stated "no" or representation elsewhere means stop, and an opt-out means stop everywhere, immediately.
3. If following up, choose the angle from what changed: new market fact, expiring quote window, a specific answer to their last open question, or an honest close-out message ("I'll stop nudging - reach out whenever it's useful") which frequently outperforms nudge four.
4. Choose channel and timing from their behavior, not habit: reply-by-text people get texts; the message lands on a weekday at a humane hour; and frequency respects the relationship stage (a hot lead can hear from you this week; a "maybe spring" seller gets a calendar entry for February, not four more touches now).
5. Draft the actual message: reference one specific detail from the real history (the home, the job, their question - proof it's not a blast), deliver the one new thing, ask one clear question with an easy answer. Three sentences is usually right. ## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The contact history, dated: every touch so far, who initiated, what was said, and what the last message promised or asked

- Signals from their side: opened-but-unanswered vs. silent, "not now" vs. nothing, any stated preference on channel or timing
- Any stop conditions in play: an opt-out, a "we went another direction," or - for real estate - signs they are now represented by another agent (which changes what's appropriate; verify obligations with your broker)
- The realistic goal of one more message: appointment, decision, or a clean close-out
- Who sends it and who should approve it before it goes

## Workflow

1. Establish where this contact actually stands, from evidence: engaged-but-busy, politely done, or genuinely undecided. The message history usually says which - three unanswered value-free check-ins reads differently than one unanswered detailed question.

Draft the actual message: reference one specific detail from the real history (the home, the job, their question - proof it's not a blast), deliver the one new thing, ask one clear question with an easy answer. Three sentences is usually right. 6. State the stop rule this memo sets: what response (or non-response) ends the sequence, and where that gets logged so the whole team honors it.

```markdown
---
name: real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-operating-plan
description: >
  Build an operating plan for client follow-up messages: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "client follow-up messages operating-plan", /real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-operating-plan.
metadata:
  category: local-services
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Business & Revenue
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-operating-plan

Build an operating plan for client follow-up messages: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "client follow-up messages operating-plan", /real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-operating-plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when follow-up should stop depending on memory: a defined touch ladder per situation (post-showing, post-consultation, post-estimate, post-closing/job), with timing, channel, message skeletons, stop rules, and CRM logging - so no lead dies of neglect and no contact gets hounded past a no. Best fit: an agent or service business that knows follow-up wins the business it's losing, but has no system carrying it.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The follow-up moments that occur weekly: post-showing feedback, post-listing-consult, post-buyer-consult, post-estimate, mid-job updates, post-closing/job-completion, long-dormant revival
- Volume per moment and who owns sending today (agent, coordinator, tech, nobody)
- The CRM or task system available for scheduling and logging - automation capability changes the design
- Existing message text in use, verbatim, and how templated it feels on arrival
- Consent and preference data held: channel opt-ins, do-not-contact flags, quiet-hours expectations (rules vary - verify for jurisdiction and channel)
- What "won" and "stopped" mean per moment, so sequences have exits

## Workflow

1. Define a ladder per moment, each rung with timing, channel, and purpose. Post-estimate example: day 2 - "any questions on the scope?" (channel they used); day 7 - one factual value-add or the validity-window reminder; day 14 - the close-out note that leaves the door open. Post-showing example: same evening - thank-you + one genuine specific about the visit; next day - feedback ask their agent etiquette allows (for buyer's-agent-to-client vs. listing-agent-to-buyer's-agent flows, keep the lanes straight - verify etiquette rules with your broker/MLS).
2. Cap every ladder with a written stop rule: a reply moves them to a live conversation (ladder ends), a no or opt-out ends all sequences immediately and flags the record, silence past the last rung moves them to the long-cycle list (quarterly, value-only touches) - not to rung one again.
3. Write the message skeletons with mandatory personalization slots: [specific detail from their file], [the one new thing], [one easy question]. The plan's rule: a skeleton with unfilled slots may not send. Skeletons carry no pressure mechanics and no assumptions about the contact beyond their stated needs.
4. Route every send through the propose-before-send gate appropriate to its risk: routine rungs (thank-yous, scheduled check-ins) use pre-approved skeleton text with filled slots reviewed by the sender; anything bespoke, sensitive, or off-ladder gets named-approver sign-off on exact text before sending. Post-closing/completion messages that ask for referrals or reviews follow their own compliance path (incentive rules vary - verify).
5. Wire the logging: every send, reply, and stop-flag lands in the CRM on the contact record, and tomorrow's due follow-ups surface in a daily ten-minute block owned by a named person. Follow-up that isn't scheduled is follow-up that doesn't happen.
6. Set quiet standards: humane hours only, frequency ceilings per contact per week across all sequences (they may be in two ladders at once - the system must see that), and channel preference honored once known.
7. Review monthly: reply rates per rung, where sequences convert or die, opt-out rate (rising means the ladders are too aggressive), and prune rungs that never produce replies.

## Output format

**Objective** - one sentence: the follow-up system and the moments it covers.

**Inputs used** - moments, volume, tooling, existing text.

**Operating plan**

| Moment | Rung | Timing | Channel | Message skeleton (slots marked) | Stop rule | Owner | Logged where |

One row-group per moment. Follow the table with: **Global rules** (quiet hours, frequency ceiling, opt-out handling, personalization-slot rule), **Approval gates** (which rungs are pre-approved skeletons vs. named-approver sends), and **Review cadence** (the monthly metrics list).

**Risks / assumptions** - including automation limits of the current CRM.

**Next action** - build one ladder (usually post-estimate or post-showing), run it two weeks, then extend.

## Do

- Give every ladder an exit and every contact one merged view - the frequency ceiling only works if sequences share it
- Make the personalization slots mandatory in the plan's own text; unfilled-slot sends are the fastest way to teach contacts to ignore the business
- Put the daily follow-up block on a named person's calendar; systems without an owner decay in three weeks
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty
- Make the result usable in ChatGPT, Grok, Claude, Cursor, Gemini, or a human workflow

## Don't

- Don't design rungs that exist only to "stay top of mind" with nothing new - value-free check-ins burn goodwill the later rungs need
- Don't let automation send anything a human hasn't approved as a skeleton, and never past an opt-out or stated no - the stop-flag check happens before every send
- Don't encode demographic assumptions into skeletons ("as a growing family, you'll love...") - slots carry the contact's stated needs and facts only
- Don't assert texting/calling consent rules - mark them "verify for your jurisdiction and channel"
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions - bespoke and sensitive messages get exact-text approval before sending

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every ladder has timing, channel, skeleton, stop rule, and owner per rung
- [ ] Opt-out and stated-no handling is immediate, global across sequences, and logged
- [ ] The frequency ceiling and quiet-hours rules apply across all ladders combined
- [ ] Skeletons contain mandatory personalization slots and no pressure mechanics or demographic assumptions
- [ ] Consent-rule specifics are marked "verify," and approval gates are defined per rung type
- [ ] The user can paste the output directly into their tool or workflow
- [ ] Assumptions and risks are visible
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-operating-plan or describe the task from the skill description.

description: >
  Review operational risk for client follow-up messages: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "client follow-up messages risk-review", /real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-risk-review.

# /real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-risk-review

Review operational risk for client follow-up messages: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "client follow-up messages risk-review", /real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-risk-review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this to audit the follow-up practice - templates, sequences, and sending habits - for the ways follow-up damages the business it's meant to grow: messages past opt-outs, pressure tactics in writing, contacting represented parties, factual claims that drift stale inside templates, and automation firing without human review. Run it before turning on any automated sequence, after a complaint, or quarterly.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Every active template and sequence, verbatim, with its trigger and timing
- How opt-outs and stated-nos are captured today, and whether automation actually checks the flag before sending
- Sending logs or CRM history for a recent period - the practice, not the policy
- For real estate: how the team handles discovering a lead is represented by another agent (obligations vary - verify with broker/MLS)
- Channel consent posture: how numbers/emails were obtained, what consent exists per channel (rules vary - verify for jurisdiction)
- Complaints or unsubscribes received, and what triggered them

## Workflow

1. Audit the stop machinery first - it is where the serious exposure lives: is there one opt-out flag visible to every sequence and every human sender? Test it against the logs: find any contact who said stop and count messages after. Each one is a finding; channel-consent legal specifics get marked "verify for your jurisdiction" rather than asserted.
2. Audit template content for claims that rot: "inventory is at record lows," "rates just dropped," "we have a crew free this week" - statements that were true when the template was written and false when it fired. Every factual claim in a template needs a freshness owner or a rewrite into evergreen form.
3. Audit for pressure mechanics and misrepresentation: invented deadlines, "another buyer is very interested" without a documented basis, urgency theater. In writing, in a discoverable channel, under a licensee's name - rate these high-impact and rewrite them into documented facts or nothing.
4. Audit for fair-housing-adjacent and assumption-driven content: templates that steer by demographic guesswork ("perfect neighborhood to raise kids in!" to selected contacts) or personalize on assumed household traits rather than stated requirements. Flag for rewrite to stated-needs-only personalization (verify wording standards with broker).
5. Audit the represented-party and lane discipline for real estate: sequences that keep soliciting a seller now listed with another brokerage, or buyer-side messages that cross agent lanes. Mark the obligation specifics "verify with broker/MLS" and flag the process gap (no representation check before send).
6. Audit the automation-human boundary: what sends with no human eyes at all, whether filled personalization slots get checked, and whether bespoke sensitive messages bypass approval. Map each sequence to its gate; "fully automatic, never reviewed" on anything non-trivial is a finding.
7. Build the risk table with likelihood/impact, split blocking (stop-machinery holes, pressure claims, represented-party sends) from advisory, assign owners and fix dates, and schedule the re-check.

## Output format

**Objective** - one sentence: the follow-up surface under review.

**Inputs used** - templates reviewed, log period sampled, consent posture.

**Risk review**

| Risk | Where it appears (template quote or log evidence) | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation (rewrite, flag fix, gate, process change) | Owner |

Group rows: Stop-machinery & consent / Stale claims / Pressure & misrepresentation / Assumption-driven personalization / Represented-party & lane discipline / Automation gates. Below the table: **Blocking findings** with immediate actions (pause sequence X until the flag check works), and **Template rewrite list** with the corrected language.

**Risks / assumptions** - including log gaps that limited the audit.

**Next action** - fix owners, sequence-pause decisions, re-check date.

## Do

- Test the opt-out flag against actual sending logs - policy review without log sampling misses the real failures

# /real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-risk-review

Review operational risk for client follow-up messages: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "client follow-up messages risk-review", /real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-risk-review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this to audit the follow-up practice - templates, sequences, and sending habits - for the ways follow-up damages the business it's meant to grow: messages past opt-outs, pressure tactics in writing, contacting represented parties, factual claims that drift stale inside templates, and automation firing without human review. Run it before turning on any automated sequence, after a complaint, or quarterly.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Every active template and sequence, verbatim, with its trigger and timing
- How opt-outs and stated-nos are captured today, and whether automation actually checks the flag before sending
- Sending logs or CRM history for a recent period - the practice, not the policy
- For real estate: how the team handles discovering a lead is represented by another agent (obligations vary - verify with broker/MLS)
- Channel consent posture: how numbers/emails were obtained, what consent exists per channel (rules vary - verify for jurisdiction)
- Complaints or unsubscribes received, and what triggered them

## Workflow

1. Audit the stop machinery first - it is where the serious exposure lives: is there one opt-out flag visible to every sequence and every human sender? Test it against the logs: find any contact who said stop and count messages after. Each one is a finding; channel-consent legal specifics get marked "verify for your jurisdiction" rather than asserted.
2. Audit template content for claims that rot: "inventory is at record lows," "rates just dropped," "we have a crew free this week" - statements that were true when the template was written and false when it fired. Every factual claim in a template needs a freshness owner or a rewrite into evergreen form.
3. Audit for pressure mechanics and misrepresentation: invented deadlines, "another buyer is very interested" without a documented basis, urgency theater. In writing, in a discoverable channel, under a licensee's name - rate these high-impact and rewrite them into documented facts or nothing.
4. Audit for fair-housing-adjacent and assumption-driven content: templates that steer by demographic guesswork ("perfect neighborhood to raise kids in!" to selected contacts) or personalize on assumed household traits rather than stated requirements. Flag for rewrite to stated-needs-only personalization (verify wording standards with broker).
5. Audit the represented-party and lane discipline for real estate: sequences that keep soliciting a seller now listed with another brokerage, or buyer-side messages that cross agent lanes. Mark the obligation specifics "verify with broker/MLS" and flag the process gap (no representation check before send).
6. Audit the automation-human boundary: what sends with no human eyes at all, whether filled personalization slots get checked, and whether bespoke sensitive messages bypass approval. Map each sequence to its gate; "fully automatic, never reviewed" on anything non-trivial is a finding.
7. Build the risk table with likelihood/impact, split blocking (stop-machinery holes, pressure claims, represented-party sends) from advisory, assign owners and fix dates, and schedule the re-check.

## Output format

**Objective** - one sentence: the follow-up surface under review.

**Inputs used** - templates reviewed, log period sampled, consent posture.

**Risk review**

| Risk | Where it appears (template quote or log evidence) | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation (rewrite, flag fix, gate, process change) | Owner |

Group rows: Stop-machinery & consent / Stale claims / Pressure & misrepresentation / Assumption-driven personalization / Represented-party & lane discipline / Automation gates. Below the table: **Blocking findings** with immediate actions (pause sequence X until the flag check works), and **Template rewrite list** with the corrected language.

**Risks / assumptions** - including log gaps that limited the audit.

**Next action** - fix owners, sequence-pause decisions, re-check date.

## Do

- Test the opt-out flag against actual sending logs - policy review without log sampling misses the real failures
- Rewrite flagged template lines on the spot; the deliverable includes the compliant text, not just the objection
- Check what fires automatically at 100% - automation without a review gate multiplies every other finding
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty
- Make the result usable in ChatGPT, Grok, Claude, Cursor, Gemini, or a human workflow

## Don't

- Don't grade pressure tactics as "salesy but fine" - undocumented urgency claims in writing are misrepresentation exposure, not style
- Don't assert consent law, do-not-call rules, or MLS solicitation rules - flag the pattern and mark "verify with broker/attorney for your jurisdiction and channel"
- Don't let a sequence keep running while its stop-machinery finding is open; the mitigation for that one is pause, not a ticket
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions - corrected templates re-enter the approval gate before returning to rotation

- [ ] Every flagged template line has replacement text supplied
- [ ] Pressure claims, stale facts, and assumption-driven personalization were each explicitly screened
- [ ] Represented-party handling was checked for real-estate flows, marked "verify with broker/MLS"
- [ ] Blocking findings pause their sequences until fixed
- [ ] The user can paste the output directly into their tool or workflow
- [ ] Assumptions and risks are visible
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate

description: >

---
# /real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-stakeholder-brief

Prepare stakeholder communication for client follow-up messages: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "client follow-up messages stakeholder-brief", /real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-stakeholder-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a proposed follow-up message needs sign-off before it goes: the draft plus the context that lets an agent, owner, or broker approve it in one read - who the contact is, the touch history, why this message now, and what could go wrong. Best fit: sensitive sends (a cold revival after months, a message near a stop-flag gray zone, a seller lead a competitor is also courting) and any message a coordinator drafted for the agent's name.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The draft message, verbatim, and the channel and send time proposed
- The contact's file: touch history with dates, last exchange, stated needs and preferences, opt-out/consent status checked and stated
- The trigger: what changed that justifies this message now (new comp, expiring quote, crew opening, long-silence revival)
- The sensitivity factors: possible representation elsewhere, past friction, gray-zone consent, competitive situation
- Who must approve: the person whose name is on the message, and the broker/owner where the sensitivity warrants it
- The stop rule if this message also gets silence

## Workflow

1. Open with the one-line situation: "Buyer lead, 4 showings in March, silent 6 weeks, opt-out clear; a comparable to the Fernwood house they loved just closed - proposing one new-info message."
2. Compress the touch history into dated bullets - every prior message, theirs and ours, one line each. The approver must see the cadence to judge whether one more is a service or a pestering.
3. Confirm the clearances explicitly: opt-out flag checked (state the result), consent for this channel (state basis; rules vary - mark "verify" where unclear), representation status for real-estate contacts (state what's known; obligations verify with broker). A brief that skips clearances isn't approvable.
4. Present the draft verbatim in a block, followed by two lines of rationale: the specific personal detail it uses, the new thing it carries, the one question it asks. If any of those three is missing from the draft, say so - that is usually the reason to revise before approving.
5. Name the failure modes honestly: reads as automated, lands during their quiet negotiation with another provider, revives a contact who wanted to be done. State the stop rule: what happens after this send if silence continues (usually: move to long-cycle or close out - not another nudge).
6. Make the ask binary with a time: approve exact text for [channel] at [time], or return edits - edits re-clear the checks before sending. Log the approval and the send on the contact record.

## Output format

**Objective** - one sentence: the contact and the send decision requested.

**Inputs used** - file reviewed, clearances run, trigger.

**Stakeholder brief**

- Situation - contact, stage, silence duration, trigger (two sentences)
- Touch history - dated one-liners, both directions
- Clearances - opt-out / channel consent / representation status, each stated with basis, "verify" marked where rules are jurisdiction-dependent
- The draft - verbatim, with channel and send time
- Why this message - the personal detail, the new thing, the one question
- Failure modes & stop rule - what could go wrong; what silence after this send triggers
- Ask - approve exact text by [time] or return edits (edits re-clear before send)

**Risks / assumptions** - including inference vs. fact in the situation read.

**Next action** - send on approval, log both, calendar the stop-rule outcome.

## Do

- Keep the whole brief under a minute's read - the touch history compression is the craft
- State clearances as checked facts with results, not as "should be fine"
- Include the do-nothing option's cost honestly; sometimes the brief's right recommendation is to hold
- Mark unknowns clearly instead of pretending certainty
- Make the result usable in ChatGPT, Grok, Claude, Cursor, Gemini, or a human workflow

## Don't

- Don't present a draft that fails the three-part test (personal detail, new thing, one question) without flagging it - the approver is approving effectiveness too
- Don't bury a gray-zone clearance in the middle of the brief; sensitivities lead, or the approval is uninformed
- Don't characterize the contact demographically or speculate about their situation beyond the file - stated facts and dated history only
- Don't assert consent/solicitation rules - mark them "verify with broker/attorney for your jurisdiction"
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't create vague inspiration-only output when the user needs an operational artifact
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions - this brief exists to enforce propose-before-send; the message never goes without the named approval

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Clearances (opt-out, consent, representation) are each stated with their checked result
- [ ] The draft appears verbatim with channel and time, and passes or is flagged on the three-part test
- [ ] The touch history is complete, dated, and compressed to one line per touch
- [ ] The stop rule for post-send silence is defined and will be logged
- [ ] The ask is binary, timed, and edits re-clear before sending
- [ ] The user can paste the output directly into their tool or workflow
- [ ] Assumptions and risks are visible
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /real-estate-local-services-follow-up-message-stakeholder-brief or describe the task from the skill description.
